title: Efficient Path Traversal
description: Practice moving up and down the filesystem using relative shortcuts.
problemStatement:
Navigate through the Linux filesystem using only relative paths and shortcuts to reach a target. This simulates moving quickly without looking at a map.

**Starting Point:** Your home directory (`~`).

**Mission:**
1.  Move to the **parent directory** of your home. (Hint: This is `/home`).
2.  Verify you are in `/home` using `pwd`.
3.  From `/home`, move to the **root** of the filesystem using a **relative** path (do not type `cd /`).
4.  Verify you are in `/`.
5.  From `/`, change to the **log** directory (`/var/log`) using an **absolute** path.
6.  From `/var/log`, move up **two** levels in one command. Where are you? (Check with `pwd`).
7.  Finally, use the 'back' button shortcut (`cd -`) twice to return to your starting home directory.
hints: Parent of `/home` is `/` (so `cd ..` from `/home` takes you to root).
To go up two levels, use `cd ../..`.
`cd -` toggles between the last two directories.
expectedOutput: Ending back in home directory after a sequence of moves.
solutionCode:
cd ..
pwd
cd ..
pwd
cd /var/log
cd ../..
pwd
cd -
cd -
